steps.text: Long ago, a small dragon named Pim lived high above {room}. {friend} settles in to listen.
Pim could not breathe fire — only puffs of warm steam. The other dragons giggled.
One night Pim saw a tiny lantern bobbing across the valley. Someone was lost.
Pim's wings trembled. The valley was {adjective} and full of shadows.
What does Pim do?
Pim flew down and puffed warm steam — a soft cloud the lost traveler could follow home.
The older dragons came roaring, but Pim's tiny voice was the one the traveler heard first. The end.
{friend} stands at the edge of a meadow. The old river song calls from far away.
The orchard smells like apples. A friendly fox tips its hat and points the way onward.
The stones are cool and smooth. {friend} hops from one to the next, counting to {count}.
Both paths meet at a wooden bridge. The river hums underneath, sleepy and slow.
{friend} sits on the bridge and listens. The river's song says: thank you for coming. The end.
{friend} found a {adjective} door at the back of {room}. It needed a key.
An old turtle whispered: the key is hidden where laughter is loudest.
{friend} listened. From the kitchen came a giggle. From the garden came a shout.
Both places were full of friends. Both were full of noise.
Which way does {friend} go?
In the kitchen, a baby mouse held the key in its tiny paws. It traded the key for a crumb of bread.
In the garden, the key dangled from a sunflower. {friend} thanked the flower with a bow. The end.
Someone has been taking one slice of cake every night. {friend} decides to investigate.
From behind the {prop}, {friend} sees the {frenemy} tiptoe in, bow politely, and pick up a slice.
Near the window, {friend} sees the {frenemy} arrive, pause to wipe its paws on the mat, and step inside.
The note says 'please share' — and in the morning, {friend} finds a tiny thank-you drawn in crumbs.
The {frenemy} isn't a thief at all — it's a baker bringing samples to taste. They share a slice together. The end.
Far above the clouds swam a whale named Drift. On her back, she carried a basket of stars.
Each night Drift placed one star in the sky. Tonight her basket was almost empty.
Only one star was left — and it was the smallest, shyest one of all.
The little star whispered: 'I'm too small. No one will see me.'
Drift smiled. 'The smallest stars,' she said, 'are the ones children wish on.'
Where does Drift place the little star?
The star settled above {room} and twinkled softly. {friend} saw it through the window and made a wish. The end.
Deep in the forest, all the sleepy animals looked up and smiled at the new little light. The end.
{friend} couldn't sleep. The night was too quiet.
A moth landed on the windowsill. 'I know a song for quiet nights,' it whispered.
Does {friend} learn the song?
{friend} hummed and the moth hummed back. The room filled with a soft, sleepy buzz. The end.
{friend} listened with eyes closing slowly. The moth sang until {friend} dreamed of warm sunlight. The end.
It's market day! {friend} has three coins and a list of one item: a {adjective} something for a friend.
The cheese mouse offers a wedge that smells like sunshine. But it costs all three coins.
The ribbon shop has every color. {friend} picks a {adjective} ribbon for two coins.
The puzzle stall has a tiny wooden box that clicks when you {action_verb} it. One coin only.
{friend} sits by the fountain and wraps the gift carefully. A friend will be very happy. The end.
{friend} discovered an attic door above {room} that no one had opened in years.
Dust hung in the air like floating sugar. The ladder creaked.
Inside the attic, {friend} found three boxes labeled 'memories,' 'maybe-someday,' and 'do not open.'
Which box does {friend} open?
Inside were photos of a younger {friend} and a tiny note: 'Remember to be brave.' {friend} smiled and tucked the note in a pocket. The end.
Inside was a folded paper kite, never flown. 'Today,' {friend} decided, 'is someday.' The end.
A small white fox lived where the snow never melted. Her favorite thing was counting flakes.
Tonight, the sky dropped flakes faster than her paws could count. She got to {count} and lost track.
{friend} arrived bundled in a scarf and offered to help count.
Together they counted slowly, two by two, until their voices got drowsy.
What happens when they reach the last flake?

choices.label: They make a tiny snow nest

steps.text: They built a soft nest of snow and curled up under the stars. The fox dreamed of summer. The end.
Steam from the cocoa fogged their noses. The fox fell asleep mid-sip, paw still wrapped around the cup. The end.
{friend} found the {needs_saving} crouched between the tomato plants. It was not moving.
On its chest blinked a tiny red light. Beside it lay a {prop} it had been holding.
{friend} read the side of the robot. It said: 'GENTLE HANDS ONLY.'
How does {friend} help?
The dirt came off in soft clouds. The robot's eyes lit up green and it bowed gently. 'Friend,' it said. The end.
The light turned blue and a tiny voice sang 'thank you.' The robot stood up and watered the tomatoes. The end.
The {guide_mentor} was the keeper of a tiny seaside lighthouse. {friend} came to visit one foggy night.
The lantern was warm and bright, but the room was full of {adjective} clutter.
On a high shelf sat a wooden box the {guide_mentor} never opened. {friend} asked about it gently.
The {guide_mentor} sighed. 'Inside is a song my mother taught me. I'm afraid I've forgotten it.'
Together they reached for the box. The lid creaked open.
What's inside?
A tiny crank turned and the song played itself. The {guide_mentor}'s eyes filled. 'There you are,' they whispered. The end.
The letter held the lyrics. The {guide_mentor} read them aloud and remembered the tune in the middle of the third line. The end.
{friend} stood before three bridges leading to a castle on a hill. Each bridge whispered a different promise.
A friendly troll asked: what has hands but cannot wave? {friend} guessed: a clock! The troll laughed and stepped aside.
Bells rang as {friend} stepped on each plank. By the end, {friend} was humming along with the bridge.
Each mirror showed {friend} a little braver than the last. The final mirror smiled and said, 'Go on, then.'
All three bridges led to the same gate. The castle was warm inside, and full of friends waiting. The end.
{friend} found a small smooth pebble that hummed when held tightly.
The pebble's hum changed depending on what {friend} thought about.
What does {friend} think about?
The pebble sang of pines and rain. {friend} closed its eyes and walked through tall green halls in its mind. The end.
The pebble hummed soft and slow, like a remembered laugh. {friend} smiled and tucked the pebble away. The end.
Past midnight, the library belonged to the old {guide_mentor}. {friend} had a special pass.
The {guide_mentor} led {friend} between the tall shelves. The books rustled softly, awake and listening.
'Tonight,' said the {guide_mentor}, 'you may pick any one book to read. But choose with your heart.'
Which book does {friend} pick?
Inside were maps to places that didn't exist yet — places {friend} would invent in dreams. The end.
It was a story of a tired bear who wanted to nap. By page three, {friend} was yawning along. The end.
{friend} flew a paper kite on a blustery hill. The string slipped — and the kite was gone.
Where does {friend} look first?
A squirrel had caught the kite and was using it as a blanket. The squirrel agreed to share — half the day each.
The kite floated like a tiny boat. A frog rode on top, happy as could be. {friend} waved hello.
The kite was tangled in tall grass. A rabbit was nibbling the string. {friend} thanked the rabbit politely.
{friend} carried the kite home, not in a hurry. The wind blew gentle now, like it was tired too. The end.
A small mouse named Ash lived in the chimney of {room}. He was very proud of his soot-grey coat.
One evening, Ash noticed something strange — his {body_part} was glowing.
Not a lot. Just a little. Like a candle had been lit inside him.
What does Ash do?
In the dark chimney, the glow lit up the whole flue. Ash was a tiny lighthouse. He felt brave. The end.
{friend} gasped softly. 'You're magic,' {friend} whispered. Ash felt nine feet tall, even though he was three inches. The end.

templates.title: The lost shoe and the mountain

steps.text: The {needs_saving} reached the foot of a tall mountain wearing only one shoe.
The other shoe had been carried off by a magpie three valleys ago.
{friend} met the {needs_saving} at a crossroads and offered to help.
How do they get up the mountain?
High on a ledge sat the nest, lined with shiny things — including the shoe. The magpie traded it for a button. The end.
{friend} and the {needs_saving} hopped one-shoe-style all the way up, laughing the whole climb. At the top: a beautiful view. The end.
The {guide_mentor} said tonight's soup would sing — but only if the right things went in. {friend} chose first.

choices.label: Mushroom and a sprig of thyme

steps.text: The soup hummed orange and sweet, like an autumn afternoon. The {guide_mentor} nodded approval.
The soup whistled in three short notes — a tune the {guide_mentor} had not heard since they were small.
The soup sang in a low, woody voice, like a tree at night. {friend} listened with eyes closed.
All the soups taste like home, the {guide_mentor} said. {friend} understood: the song was made of love. The end.
{friend} folded a tiny paper boat and set it in the gutter after a rainstorm.
The boat sailed away faster than expected. {friend} jogged alongside, watching.
At the corner, the boat met a leaf — and a tiny crew climbed aboard.
Three ladybugs and one beetle. They saluted {friend} solemnly.
What happens at the storm drain?
The crew disembarked, bowed, and marched off into the grass on important ladybug business. The end.
The boat slipped into the dark and away. {friend} imagined oceans for it. Maybe rivers. Maybe further. The end.

templates.title: The baker who woke the sun

steps.text: Long before sunrise, the {quest_giver} kneaded dough in a warm dark kitchen.
The {quest_giver} had a secret: every morning, the smell of baking bread woke the sun itself.
But this morning, the {quest_giver} overslept. The sun had not risen.
{friend} ran to the bakery to help.
What do they bake?
The smell rose up like a hug. The sun yawned, stretched, and climbed into the sky pink and pleased. The end.
Crusty and golden — the sun woke gently and smiled at the world. {friend} got the first warm slice. The end.
{friend} found a hidden door behind the {prop} in {room}. It only opened when you whispered.
The door swung open onto a sunlit garden where every flower glowed. {friend} stepped inside, careful not to wake the bees.
The door creaked and showed a long hallway lined with books. Each one knew the answer to something.
The door opened on a quiet room where someone {friend} had not seen in a long time was waiting with cocoa.
Whatever lay inside, it was meant for {friend}. The door always finds the right somewhere. The end.
Low tide opened a secret sea cave below the cliffs. {friend} climbed down with a tide chart and a sense of wonder.
The pool sparkled with little fish that shone like stars. They circled {friend}'s reflection like a constellation.
The wind carried a song that the cave made by itself, with no instrument. {friend} hummed along.
Both paths met at a small wooden chest. Inside: a single perfect shell, smooth as a tooth.
{friend} held the shell to its ear. The sea whispered: come back any time. The end.
Tired and a little lost, {friend} stood before three doors in a strange village.
Behind the blue door: a kitchen full of the smell of bread. An old voice said: come in, come in.
Behind the green door: a garden of moonflowers nodding open. A cat waved with one slow paw.
Behind the yellow door: a room of soft cushions and a lamp turned low. Someone was already saving a seat.
Whichever door {friend} chose, it led to the same warm table — and the same kindly {helper_townsperson} who knew {friend}'s name. The end.
{friend} found a smooth flat pebble and stood by the pond, deciding what to do.
Plink, plink, plink — three skips! The pond clapped quietly with little ripples. The end.
The pebble sank slow and soft, and the rings spread out like a sleepy hello. The end.
{friend} yawned. The cat across the room yawned. The owl outside yawned too.
Where does the yawn go next?
The moon caught the yawn and tucked it into its smile. Goodnight. The end.
The river carried the yawn out to sea, where every wave nodded off. The end.
{friend} found a small {adjective} button on the floor of {room}. It seemed to wink.
What does {friend} wish?
A tiny crumb of cake appeared in {friend}'s palm. Just enough. {friend} grinned. The end.
A short hum, three notes long, danced through the room. {friend} hummed it back. The end.
The {needs_saving} got lost from its sky and bumped softly against {friend}'s window.
{friend} cracked the window open. The cloud floated in, shy and damp.
How does {friend} help the cloud get home?
A breeze rolled through and the cloud rose up, waved a soft puff goodbye, and drifted home. The end.
{friend} sang gently. The cloud lifted, listened, and floated back to the sky humming along. The end.
{friend} lost one mitten on the way to the porch. Only one tiny {body_part} stayed warm.
Where does {friend} look?
The mitten was tucked beneath the {prop}, perfectly folded. A mouse had used it as a sleeping bag. The end.
The mitten waited behind the door, pretending to nap. {friend} put it on with a happy hum. The end.
Once a small puzzle box appeared on {friend}'s shelf. It would not open for hands.
It would not open for keys. It would not open for words.
But the box softened when {friend} pressed it gently with a {body_part}.
Inside lay a small note: 'You knew the answer all along — listen with your whole self.'
What does {friend} do with the note?

choices.label: Read it aloud one more time

steps.text: {friend} slept with the note close. In dreams, the box opened a hundred more times, each holding a softer secret. The end.
Saying the words made them feel true. {friend} folded the note neatly and went out into the world a little braver. The end.
On a quiet hill stood a small chapel with exactly {count} candles flickering in its windows.
Each candle was lit by a different friend. Each carried a wish.
{friend} climbed the hill with a candle of its own to add.
What is {friend}'s wish?
{friend} placed the candle with the others. Its flame leaned toward the sunrise, ready. The end.
The flame burned softer than the rest, but warmer somehow. Somewhere far away, a friend felt unexpectedly held. The end.
Once upon a time, {friend} could do almost anything except {action_verb}.
An old {guide_mentor} on a stone bridge said: 'There are two ways to learn. Patient and brave. Both work.'
Which way does {friend} try?
Day after day, {friend} practiced for ten minutes. By the end of the month, it became natural.
{friend} tripped, fell, got back up, tripped, fell, got back up. By sundown, {friend} was doing it without thinking.
Both ways led {friend} back to the bridge. The {guide_mentor} bowed deeply. 'Now,' it said, 'teach a friend.' The end.
{friend} owned two things it loved: a {prop} and another {prop}. They were jealous of each other.
Each one wanted to be {friend}'s favorite. {friend} did not know what to do.
An old {guide_mentor} laughed gently. 'Two things can be loved at once,' she said. 'Watch.'
She placed both {prop}s side by side and told them a story about belonging.
How does the story end for the {prop}s?
The two {prop}s leaned together and never argued again. {friend} carried both proudly. The end.
They learned to share the shelf without bickering. {friend} loved both equally — and they finally believed it. The end.
One morning, {friend} woke up to find {room} entirely {adjective}. Even the dust was {adjective}.
Nothing was missing. Nothing was broken. It was just... very {adjective}.
How does {friend} respond?
{friend} discovered a sleepy {trickster} in the closet. She had spilled her bottle of feelings. By teatime, things were normal again.
{friend} spent the day in the {adjective} {room}, soaking it in. It faded by sundown, but the memory stayed bright. The end.
Far away in a village called Plinth, there was one day each year when everyone had to {action_verb} together.
{friend} arrived in Plinth on exactly that day, a stranger and a little nervous.
The {quest_giver} handed {friend} a small ribbon. 'You'll need this,' she said. 'It helps.'
How does {friend} join in?
A kind farmer offered an arm. Together they {action_verb}'d in perfect rhythm and laughed the whole time. The end.
{friend} {action_verb}'d in the middle of the square with quiet confidence. The whole village cheered. The end.
When {friend} woke up, there was a map drawn lightly on its {body_part}, in faint silver lines.
It showed a path through {room}, ending at a place {friend} had never noticed before.
Does {friend} follow the map?
The map led to a small loose floorboard. Beneath it: a tin of letters from someone who used to live here. The end.
By the time {friend} finished toast, the lines had faded. But {friend} remembered every turn — and found the spot. The end.
Past the edge of town, just past the old fence, a circus tent appeared only on nights without a moon.
{friend} found the entrance by accident, chasing a runaway balloon.
A small {trickster}, half rabbit, half something else, asked: 'Do you have a story?'
{friend} told a small one — about a lost shoe and a found friend. The {trickster} nodded, satisfied.
Inside, the air smelled like cinnamon and something a little older. A row of seats waited.
The first act was three foxes balancing teacups. The teacups were full. Nothing spilled.
The second act was a very tall bear who recited a poem about wind.
What is the final act?
Stars hung in the tent like ornaments. Two acrobats swung from one to the next, bowing as the lights twinkled. {friend} clapped softly. The end.
A kitten walked the entire length of a yarn tightrope, never wobbling. The crowd held its breath. {friend} did too. The end.
The {quest_giver} and her crew set out across the Quiet Sea looking for the singing islands.
{friend} was the smallest sailor on board, in charge of the lookout perch.
On the third day they passed a fog so thick the ship sailed by ear alone.
On the seventh day, a whale offered them directions in a slow, kind voice.
On the tenth day, {friend} spotted a green smudge on the horizon. 'Land!'
Which island do they visit first?
The island sang in deep, warm tones. The crew danced slowly along the beach.
Tiny bells hung from every tree. A breeze made the whole island chime. {friend} laughed in delight.
Even the stones hummed faintly. {friend} sat down and listened until the sun dipped low.
All three islands were waiting for them eventually. The crew sailed home with a song in every pocket. The end.
In the back room of an old shop, {friend} found a tiny clockwork bird, perfectly still.
Its wings were silver, its eyes were two tiny seeds. A note read: 'Wind once, then wait.'
{friend} turned the small key on its back. Tick. Tick. Tick.
The bird's eyes blinked. It tilted its head, looking at {friend} as if remembering.
It hopped to the windowsill. It looked at the sky. Then it looked back.
It wanted to go. But it also wanted to stay. The note had not said which.
What does {friend} do?
The bird flew once around {friend}'s head, dipped low in thanks, and zipped out into the bright air. {friend} smiled. The end.
The bird settled in, fluffed its silver wings, and sang one slow note before falling still. A keeper, then. The end.
Deep in a forest no map showed, every tree knew the name of someone who once stood beneath it.
{friend} entered with a small list of friends to find.
The first tree was a willow, who whispered the name of {friend}'s grandmother.
The second was an oak, who whispered the name of an old teacher.
The third was a small birch with a name {friend} hadn't expected to hear.
It was {friend}'s own name — though much younger, when first the trees had heard it.
How does {friend} respond?
{friend} rested against the trunk. The birch hummed softly all afternoon, as if it had missed {friend} too. The end.
{friend} whispered a friend's name into the birch's bark. Years from now, that friend would arrive and feel suddenly welcome. The end.

templates.title: The hot air balloon to nowhere

steps.text: {friend} climbed into a basket beneath an enormous striped balloon. The pilot was a grumpy {frenemy} named Felix.
'Where to?' Felix asked, lighting the burner. {friend} did not know.
'Then nowhere,' Felix grunted. 'Nowhere is my favorite place.'
Up they went. Up and up, until the houses became small and the rivers became ribbons.
Felix passed {friend} a thermos of cocoa. They drifted in silence for a while.
What does {friend} ask Felix?

choices.label: What he sees from up here

steps.text: Felix smiled — the first smile of the trip. 'Because nowhere never asks you to be anyone in particular.'
Felix pointed slowly: a heron, a fox kit, a kid waving from a yard. 'Everything,' he said. 'I see everything.'
Hours later they touched down in a meadow. {friend} hopped out feeling lighter than the basket itself. The end.
The {quest_giver}'s {needs_saving} went missing in {room}. {friend} agreed to help search.
Behind the {prop}, {friend} found tiny prints leading toward a vent. The trail was warm.
On a high shelf, two yellow eyes blinked at {friend} from a pile of soft sweaters.
By the food bowl, the {needs_saving} had simply been napping the whole time, ear twitching.
{friend} carried the {needs_saving} back to the {quest_giver}, who was overjoyed and gave {friend} a slice of pie. The end.
On the night of the lantern festival, every house gave {friend} one paper lantern to release.
From the bridge, the lantern joined a hundred others drifting along the river, glowing soft and steady.
From the hill, the lantern rose into the dark sky, climbing slowly until it joined the stars.
Wherever it went, {friend}'s wish went with it. The night smelled like honey and woodsmoke. The end.
Three squirrels held a contest each year. This year, {friend} was invited to be the judge.
The squirrels lined up their piles. The biggest pile belonged to the smallest squirrel — Beanie. {friend} clapped.
One squirrel leapt from a low branch. Another from a high one. A third just leapt straight up. All three wobbled and grinned.
Each squirrel showed {friend} a hiding spot. {friend} promised to keep all three secret, even from the wind.
{friend} gave every squirrel a tiny ribbon. 'Three winners,' {friend} declared. The squirrels chittered with joy. The end.
An old {quest_giver} in the meadow only gave water to those who solved its daily riddle. Today's riddle: a {adjective} thing that grows when shared.
The {quest_giver} bubbled and said, 'Yes — and also.' It accepted the answer kindly.
The {quest_giver} laughed back, water splashing high. 'A clever one,' it murmured.
The {quest_giver} hummed in agreement. 'A musician's well today, then.'
{friend} drew up a bucket of cool, clear water and shared it with thirsty friends back home. The end.
After dark, the garden behind {room} woke up. The flowers walked. {friend} had to choose where to wander.
The roses poured tiny dewdrop teas. {friend} sipped one and felt very fancy.
The sunflower gave a long speech on the proper angle of leaves. {friend} took polite notes on a leaf.
Just before the moon set, all the flowers bowed and froze still. {friend} crept back to bed, smiling. The end.
A traveling {trickster} offered {friend} three balloons. Only one held a true wish.
Red was warm and bright. {friend} held it carefully and felt its small weight.
Yellow was light and quick. {friend} held its string and felt the breeze tug.
Blue was cool and quiet. {friend} held it and felt strangely seen.
When {friend} released the balloon, the sky brightened with one tiny new star. The {trickster} winked. They were all true, after all. The end.
A letter addressed to {friend} arrived a full day early. The postmark was tomorrow's.
Inside was a note: 'You will be brave today.' {friend} squared its shoulders, ready for whatever came.
{friend} placed the letter under the lamp and went about the day. The waiting felt patient and warm.
By afternoon, {friend} understood: the bravery was in choosing what to do with a note from the future. Either way was right. The end.
Somebody was stealing lullabies from the village. By bedtime, no one could remember a single tune.
{friend} hummed a brand new tune very softly. The small velvet {trickster} crept out and tried to gather it up.
Past midnight, {friend} heard the softest rustling and saw the small velvet {trickster} scooping up an old lullaby like a leaf.
The {trickster} wasn't stealing — it had been collecting lost ones to bring back. It returned every tune by morning. The end.

templates.title: The train that only ran on Tuesdays

steps.text: A small train arrived at the platform every Tuesday afternoon and only on Tuesdays. {friend} climbed aboard.
Outside the window, the world slowly turned to a watercolor — soft and just a little blurred. {friend} pressed its nose to the glass.
In the dining car, a fox waiter served tea and a slice of clouds-and-cream pie. {friend} took small grateful bites.
The train stopped at a sunlit village neither on any map. {friend} stepped out and waved as it pulled away. The end.
The polite {guide_mentor} invited {friend} to tea at the top of a hill. {friend} brought a small gift.
The {guide_mentor} spread the jam on a piece of dragon toast. 'You have excellent taste,' she rumbled, pleased.
The {guide_mentor} read three poems aloud, her voice deep and slow. {friend} listened with both ears wide open.
The {guide_mentor} tucked the mint into her teapot. 'A perfect afternoon needs a perfect garnish,' she said.
They sat together as the sun lowered, sipping tea and watching the valley turn gold. {friend} promised to come again. The end.

templates.title: The thunder pup learns to bark

steps.text: Up in the rainy {room}, a thunder pup paced in tiny, worried circles.
The pup's tiny growls came out like soft thunder rolls. {friend} crouched to listen.
How does {friend} cheer the pup on?
{friend} barked once, the pup answered with a rumble, and the clouds clapped politely. The end.
The pup tried a long howl. The sky answered back, low and friendly. The end.

templates.title: The ladder that climbed the rain

steps.text: A silver ladder appeared in the puddle outside {room}. {friend} blinked at it.
The {quest_giver} said softly: 'Climb only if you climb gently — the rain is sleeping.'
{friend} climbed step by step, each rung a cool, quiet rain-drop holding still.
At the top, a small cloud yawned and tucked {friend} into a dream of softer rain. The end.

templates.title: The morning fog forgot its way

steps.text: Outside {room}, a patch of fog stood very still on the steps.
The fog had wandered too far from the river and forgotten which way back.
How does {friend} help?
{friend} hummed low and slow. The fog drifted gently, following the tune home.
{friend} pointed with a {prop}. The fog rolled along the path, grateful and quiet.
{friend} watched a fawn made of frost step out from under the porch.
It tried one tiny dance step. A crystal hoof made the smallest tinkle.
{friend} clapped softly — just enough that the fawn knew it had a friend.

templates.title: The hail that turned to marbles

steps.text: {friend} stepped onto the porch after the storm and looked down.
The hail that fell on Tuesday rolled — and bounced — like {count} bright marbles.
What does {friend} do with the marbles?
{friend} built a wobbly little tower. It stood for one bright minute, then giggled apart.
The marbles rolled in a perfect circle, then melted into a cool puddle, still smiling.

templates.title: The sunbeam that broke in two

steps.text: A sunbeam came in through {room} and broke neatly in two on the floor.
The {guide_mentor} whispered: 'Beams break gently when they want to share.'
{friend} held out a {body_part}. One half of the beam settled there, warm.
The other half curled into a corner like a cat. Both halves hummed. The end.
Behind the couch, the dust bunnies were holding their monthly council.
They wore tiny acorn-cap hats. They argued about which corner was coziest.
{friend} crouched quietly so as not to spook the council.
How does {friend} take part?
{friend} whispered hello. The dust bunnies bowed and made room. They voted unanimously.
{friend} slid a {prop} forward. The bunnies hopped on it like a stage and gave a speech.
On a stone behind {room}, {friend} found a perfect circle of moss with four green points.
It pointed not to north but to wherever a friend was thinking of you.
The {guide_mentor} said: 'Hold it gently. Most directions deserve a quiet voice.'
{friend} held it. The needle pointed home. {friend} smiled and stepped back inside. The end.

templates.title: The breeze that needed a kite

steps.text: The wind through the trees sounded a little lonely today.
A small breeze drifted up to {friend} and whispered: 'I lost my kite. I feel empty.' The {needs_saving} breeze wobbled.
How does {friend} help the breeze find its kite?

choices.label: Borrow a leaf as a kite

steps.text: {friend} folded a paper kite. The {needs_saving} breeze swept it up, and they spun together, joyful.
{friend} offered a wide leaf. The {needs_saving} breeze grabbed it and danced across the yard.
{friend} built a snow dog at the edge of the path. It had stick ears and pebble eyes.
The snow dog promised, in a whisper only {friend} could hear, to guard the porch all night.
{friend} patted its cold head and went inside. The snow dog stood watch, very proud, very still.
Down in the bay, a strand of kelp had woven itself into a soft green cradle.
The {guide_mentor} sea-turtle said: 'It rocks any small thing that needs rocking.'
{friend} placed a sleepy seashell inside. The kelp swayed in a slow, easy rhythm.
The shell snored a tiny shell-snore. {friend} smiled and let the tide rock them both. The end.
Beneath the pier, a tiny post office had opened. Mailboxes were pearls, doors were shells.
The {quest_giver} octopus stamped each pearl with a polite sucker-print.
{friend} had a small letter to send — a thank-you to last summer.
How does {friend} send the letter?

choices.label: Whisper it to a passing fish

steps.text: The clamshell hummed once and zipped through the current. The reply came back wrapped in seaweed ribbon.
A silver fish carried the message in its memory. It returned with a soft 'you're welcome' bubble.

templates.title: The bird who lived in the anchor

steps.text: An old anchor on the dock had a small round hole, and inside lived a very brave little bird.
{friend} brought a crumb of {prop} and set it carefully on the rim.
The bird chirped twice — once for thanks, once for tomorrow — and tucked itself back in. The end.
Out on the rocks, something tiny was sniffling.
A small pirate crab, who was the {needs_saving}, had dropped a single salt tear into the tidepool.
How does {friend} comfort the crab?
{friend} placed the pebble in the crab's claw. It clicked once, gratefully, and tucked it away.
{friend} hummed a low tide song. The crab's eyes softened. It bowed and scuttled home.
Down at the cove, a crab in a tiny tricorn hat tapped a clipboard.
The crab mayor, who was the {quest_giver}, needed a hand reading the day's tide-mail.
How does {friend} help the mayor?
{friend} sorted them by sparkle. The mayor declared {friend} an honorary deputy with a tiny seaweed ribbon.
{friend} read each headline in a brave voice. The mayor nodded along, claws clapping politely.
Every full moon, the tide leaves one small gift on the beach for whoever finds it first.
The {quest_giver} gull called: 'Listen for the smallest shine — that's the one for you.'
{friend} walked slowly. Most shells sang. One sang in a smaller, kinder voice.
{friend} picked it up gently. The shell whispered a thank-you-for-noticing. The end.
The {quest_giver} whale carried an old rolled-up charter in a barnacle pouch on its side.
On the charter were rules: be kind to small fish, share the warm currents, hum on Tuesdays.
But one rule had washed clean over the centuries. No one remembered which.
{friend} traced the empty line with a careful finger.
What does {friend} write for the missing rule?
The whale read it aloud. A school of fish in the distance immediately waved their tails in agreement.
The whale considered. Then it laughed a long deep laugh that shook the kelp and made every shell sing.
Down a quiet ladder, {friend} stepped onto a sandy floor between waving green pages.
The seaweed library was open only at twilight. Books were long green ribbons that swayed.
Which book does {friend} read?
{friend} learned that every lost mitten has a sister mitten waiting somewhere kind. Comforting news.
{friend} learned about a thousand tiny kindnesses the ocean had done without telling anyone.

templates.title: The shell that played the radio

steps.text: {friend} found a curly shell tucked behind the porch step.
Pressed to {friend}'s ear, the shell played a faint old radio show from somewhere far at sea.
Which station does {friend} tune to?
Three crabs took turns telling jokes. {friend} laughed so hard the shell nearly tipped over.
A gull declared the forecast: 'Sunny with a chance of polite waves.' {friend} cheered loudly.
The pirate, the {frenemy}, had borrowed {friend}'s map and returned it slightly soggy.
The pirate stood at the door clutching a tiny bouquet of seaweed in apology.
{friend} blinked. The pirate's eye-patch was on backwards from nerves.
Behind the pirate, a parrot quietly cleared its throat.
How does {friend} accept the apology?

choices.label: Offer a warm cup of cocoa

steps.text: {friend} poured cocoa into two mugs. The pirate sighed with relief and stayed for a long, warm story.
Together they smoothed the map flat with two careful hands. By the end it looked even prettier than before.

templates.title: The comet courier delivers a parcel

steps.text: A small comet wearing a courier sash zoomed up to the window of {room}.
The {quest_giver} comet had one parcel left and the address was smudged.
{friend} squinted. The label glittered: ... someone who listens.
How does {friend} help deliver it?

choices.label: Pass it along to the next neighbor

steps.text: Inside was a single song-note, wrapped in stardust. The comet beamed: that was the address. {friend} kept it safe.
{friend} walked it next door. The {helper_townsperson} cat purred and the comet vanished, satisfied.

templates.title: The bakery on the dark side of the moon

steps.text: On the dark side of the moon, a bakery opens only when someone on Earth feels a little hungry.
Tonight, {friend} felt hungry. The bakery's chimney puffed a small silver puff.
The {quest_giver} moon-baker leaned out the window: 'What does a small hungry heart want?'
{friend} thought carefully. Carefully is the right way to think about night-bread.
What does {friend} order?
It tasted like the smell of clean sheets. {friend} ate half and saved half for a friend.
The moon-jam was sweet and small and somehow tasted like remembering. {friend} hummed for an hour.

templates.title: The planet that needed a bed

steps.text: A tired small planet, drifting in the dark, asked {friend} where to put its head down.
{friend} pointed gently at the soft black space between two friendly stars.
The planet settled there with a yawn. The stars tucked it in with their warm light. The end.

templates.title: The cat who napped on an asteroid

steps.text: From the porch telescope, {friend} spotted a tiny grey blob orbiting nearby.
A small grey cat had found the perfect napping spot — a smooth asteroid spinning very, very slowly.
How does {friend} say hello without waking the cat?
{friend} drifted a pillow alongside. The cat sighed in its sleep, snuggled deeper, purred in zero-g.
{friend} hummed. The cat's whiskers twitched in time, but it kept dreaming of warmer planets.
It glittered faintly even in the daytime.
The {quest_giver} gave {friend} a tiny bottle of stardust borrowed from a kind nebula.
How does {friend} use the dust?

choices.label: Save it for a cloudy day

steps.text: Tonight's dreams were softer and brighter. In one, a moon waved. {friend} woke happy.
{friend} tucked the bottle on a shelf. Later, when the day felt grey, a single shake brought back wonder.
A tiny rocket no bigger than a teaspoon parked itself on {friend}'s windowsill.
It had {count} letters in its tail-fin, all addressed to people on this very street.
{friend} delivered them gently door to door, knocking softly each time.
The rocket beeped a small thank-you and zipped back into the morning sky. The end.

templates.title: The scarf knitted from a nebula

steps.text: Out of soft purple-pink threads, a {guide_mentor} had knitted a scarf for {friend}.
The scarf wrapped warm and slow. It smelled faintly of clean cold space.
{friend} wore it on the porch. Tiny stars winked from inside the weave.
The night settled gently. {friend} stayed wrapped and watched the dark turn kind. The end.

templates.title: The song that orbited a planet

steps.text: Astronomers had been arguing politely about it for weeks.
Once a song went into orbit around a small green planet and refused to come down.
What does {friend} do?
{friend} lay on the grass. The song came overhead once an hour, low and clear. {friend} smiled with eyes closed.
{friend} hummed. The song dipped, met the hum, and the two danced together in slow loops.
Outside the porthole, a small moon waved.
The {quest_giver} astronaut invited {friend} aboard for tea. The teacup floated. The cookies floated.
How does {friend} drink it?
{friend} caught a perfectly round blob with a careful straw. It tasted like Earth-summer. {friend} grinned.
{friend} bobbled a cookie out of the air with both hands. The astronaut applauded the gentle catch.
A solar sail no bigger than a kerchief sailed up to {friend}'s window with a tiny request.
It wanted a name. It had been catching sunlight all year without one.
The {quest_giver} sun gently warmed the sail. The sail glowed back, patient.
{friend} thought of names — kind ones, bright ones, soft ones.
What does {friend} name the sail?
The sail puffed proudly. From then on, it left small bright trails wherever it sailed. The end.
The sail folded around the name like a hug. It sailed off, gentle and shining. The end.
It needed oil. It needed a friend.
The {frenemy} knight rode up on a small rusty pony that creaked at every step.
How does {friend} help?

choices.label: Polish the pony with a soft cloth
Sing the pony a courage song

steps.text: {friend} rubbed gently. The pony shone like new. It bowed its head, grateful. The knight nodded once.
{friend} sang. The pony's creaks turned to clinks, then to a happy clop-clop down the road.
Up the hill, a small castle stood completely covered in soft, sleepy moss.
{friend} climbed the spiral stair, each step a green cushion that whispered welcome.
At the top, the wind hummed quietly through every window. {friend} sat down and listened.

templates.title: The kind kraken at the moat

steps.text: Castle messengers tiptoed across the bridge.
A kraken had moved into the castle moat. It was nervous. It waved each tentacle politely.
How does {friend} make the kraken feel welcome?
The kraken accepted with two tentacles and a curtsy. It tucked the bread away for later.
The swans inspected the kraken, declared it acceptable, and offered it a feathered scarf.
The shopkeeper smiled and offered it for one polite hello.
In a quiet shop, {friend} found a sword the size of a teaspoon. Its hilt was embroidered with jam-flowers.
How does {friend} use it?

choices.label: Hang it on the kitchen wall

steps.text: The porridge swirled into a shape like a friendly dragon, then ate itself politely. {friend} laughed.
It looked very brave up there. Every passing spoon nodded to it respectfully.

templates.title: The paper banner that flew highest

steps.text: At the castle parade, a tiny paper banner sagged at the back of the procession.
The {needs_saving} banner was the smallest by far and felt very small indeed.
{friend} fixed the string and lifted it high so the wind could find it properly.
The wind found it and the banner climbed higher than any flag that day. The end.
A young squire showed {friend} a row of jars on a windowsill, each holding a different puddle.
'This one's from a thunderstorm. This one's from a slow drizzle. This one's from a happy splash.'
{friend} bowed politely. The jars glittered. The squire promised one day to lend out the brave thunder-jar.

templates.title: The jester who borrowed a helmet

steps.text: A bee buzzed in concerned circles around the helmet.
The {trickster} jester had borrowed a knight's helmet, three sizes too big. It clanked at every step.
How does {friend} make it fit?
The hay puffed out the eye-slit cheerfully. The jester tumbled in a perfect cartwheel. The court applauded.
Every time the jester tilted, the bell jingled. The whole castle giggled for hours.

templates.title: The drawbridge that loved a rock

steps.text: The castle drawbridge lowered very slowly, because each evening it waited to see a particular small rock.
The {guide_mentor} explained: 'They have been friends a long time, those two.'
{friend} sat down between them and listened to the bridge creak hello and the rock hum back.
The night came quietly. The bridge stayed open just long enough for one more kind word. The end.
{friend} climbed the spiral stair to the highest turret. An owl waited, holding a tiny book in a wing.
The owl read three lines: 'The moon. The mouse. The kindly stone.' Then it closed the book.
{friend} smiled. The owl tucked its head under its wing. {friend} tiptoed down to bed.

templates.title: The mitten that became a gauntlet

steps.text: Sunlight glinted off its yarn like polished steel.
A red mitten woke up dreaming it was a gauntlet. It sat up on the dresser, shiny-eyed.
How does {friend} honor the dream?

choices.label: Make it a tiny knight's coat
Knight it with a wooden spoon

steps.text: {friend} stitched a cape from a napkin. The mitten stood proudly all day, every finger a flag.
{friend} tapped each thumb softly. 'Arise, brave mitten.' The mitten saluted. The kitchen cheered.

templates.title: The tugboat that dreamed of stars

steps.text: The harbor tugboat puttered along, towing barges, all year long.
But every night the tugboat dreamed it was a small rocket, gliding among quiet stars.
The {quest_giver} harbormaster overheard the dream and told {friend}.
{friend} climbed aboard one evening to listen.
How does {friend} help the dream?

choices.label: Paint a tiny star on the bow
Tie a paper rocket to the smokestack

steps.text: The next morning the tug felt secretly cosmic. It towed everything with a new gentle glide.
The wind made the paper rocket flutter. The tug whistled twice — happy, and a little proud.

templates.title: The candle on the midnight trolley

steps.text: {friend} stepped aboard at the very last stop.
The midnight trolley carried one candle in a glass jar, lit just bright enough for {count} sleepy passengers.
How does {friend} ride?
Outside, the dark turned soft. The candle reflected on the glass like a steady, kind eye. {friend} dozed.
{friend} watched the small flame nod with every gentle sway. The trolley hummed a low, kind song.

templates.title: The bicycle bell that learned a song

steps.text: A red bicycle bell had been quiet all winter, but one morning it began to ring a small new tune.
It was {adjective}, slow and cheerful, three notes long.
{friend} rang it at each corner of the block. Neighbors waved from their porches.
By the end of the ride, the whole street was humming the little tune. The end.

templates.title: The pillow that traveled by train

steps.text: The conductor said: 'Window seat or aisle?'
A small soft pillow had a ticket of its own. The conductor punched it with a tiny careful punch.
Where does {friend} ride with the pillow?
The quiet car hummed. The pillow leaned against {friend}'s shoulder. They watched the dusk roll by.
Plates clinked softly. The pillow listened to {count} polite conversations. Every passenger smiled at it.
The {quest_giver} owned a small balloon that delivered letters to anyone above the treetops.
Today's letter was for a kite who had been very lonely.
{friend} climbed into the basket. The balloon sighed up off the lawn.
Below, the {sidekick} dog ran in cheerful circles waving a paw goodbye.
How does {friend} deliver the letter?

choices.label: Tie it to the kite's string
Read it aloud as they fly past

steps.text: The kite read it in the wind and dipped twice — a yes. The balloon and the kite waltzed back to ground.
{friend} read kindly. The kite hummed along. They all parted as friends, with promises of next week.

templates.title: The scooter that followed a leaf

steps.text: The breeze called gently from the maple tree.
A single bright leaf skidded down the sidewalk. {friend}'s scooter rolled out, curious.
Where does the leaf take {friend}?

choices.label: Around the corner to the park

steps.text: The leaf danced over the curb. {friend} followed, laughing. They circled the park three perfect times.
The leaf hopped onto the railing. {friend} parked. They watched the stream below carry a sister leaf onward.
The sub puttered between two rubber ducks like passing islands.
A tiny submarine, tinier than a pea, motored across {friend}'s bath water. Inside sat one nervous {needs_saving}, a bean.
How does {friend} steady the bean?
{friend} gently nudged with a {prop}. The sub leveled out. The bean tipped a tiny bean-bow.
{friend} sang muffled bubbles. The bean stood at the periscope and felt very, very brave.
Early in the morning, a snowplow rumbled past {room}. A fox rode shyly in the passenger seat.
The driver waved. The fox waved with one orange paw. {friend} waved back from the window.
The plow cleared the road in a tidy white curve, and the fox supervised with quiet authority.
By breakfast the street was clean. The fox tipped an imaginary hat at {friend}. The end.
The {quest_giver} farmer beamed and waved a folded handkerchief.
At the country fair, a small red tractor rolled down the road wearing {count} ribbons.
How does {friend} cheer?

choices.label: Sing along to the parade tune

steps.text: Petals swirled. The tractor honked a polite small honk and tipped its smokestack like a hat.
{friend} sang. The whole crowd joined. The tractor blushed bright crimson under its ribbons.

templates.title: The reed who rode the ferry

steps.text: A long thin reed had paid its fare with a single bright pebble. The ferry captain bowed politely.
{friend} sat beside it on the railing. The reed bent gently with each wave, nodding hello to ducks.
When the ferry docked, the reed thanked everyone and walked itself ashore on its own quiet ripple.
Under the porch, a hedgehog had built itself a tiny reading lamp out of one firefly and a leaf shade.
The {guide_mentor} hedgehog turned a small page with a careful spike.
{friend} crouched and listened. The book was about three brave acorns who became a fence.
When the chapter ended, the firefly winked twice, and the hedgehog curled into a polite goodnight. The end.
{count} pairs of green eyes turned politely toward {friend}.
Down by the pond, the frog choir was rehearsing — and one note kept coming out as a hiccup.
How does {friend} help?
{friend} waved a {prop} like a baton. The choir followed along. The hiccup turned into a beautiful low note.
{friend} hummed gently. The choir matched the hum, and the whole pond filled with one warm chord.
In the park, a polite badger had borrowed a bench for the afternoon. It sat reading a folded newspaper.
{friend} sat at the other end. The badger nodded. {friend} nodded back. They both watched the leaves.
When the badger left, it tipped a small acorn-cap hat in thanks. The bench felt a little warmer somehow.
The owl's needles clicked like soft tiny bells.
An owl in the rafters of {room} knit mittens in pairs of two for everyone who wore four paws.
Who does {friend} introduce to the owl?
The cat tried on the mittens, looked grumpy, then secretly loved them. It purred all winter.
The raccoon tried each color, did a careful catwalk twirl, and tipped a tiny hat to thank the owl.

templates.title: The otter who carried the river

steps.text: Once a river dried up overnight, and a small otter was the only one who remembered how it sounded.
The {quest_giver} otter carried a single drop of river-song in its mouth across the meadow.
{friend} walked beside the otter, very quietly so as not to spill the song.
Every step, the meadow grass leaned in to listen.
Where does {friend} suggest the otter pour the drop?
The riverbed filled, slow and bright. By dusk, the river was running again, humming its old tune.
A new pond shimmered into being. Frogs and dragonflies arrived within the hour to introduce themselves.
The card was creased and well-loved.
The {quest_giver} raven had a secret recipe written on a tiny shiny card.
How does {friend} help cook?
{friend} stirred. The pot let out a small, fragrant cloud. The raven sniffed and bowed approval.
{friend} placed the {prop} in. The whole pot shimmered. The raven cawed a quiet 'excellent.'
A turtle invited {friend} to a picnic that would, the turtle warned politely, take all afternoon.
They unpacked very slowly: one strawberry, one cracker, one small tin of lemonade.
Each bite was savored for {count} long, sunny minutes. {friend} left full and slow-hearted and happy.

templates.title: The fox who outgrew its sweater

steps.text: The fox shuffled its paws and looked at the floor.
A fox, the {needs_saving}, had grown taller over the summer. Its red sweater was now embarrassingly short.
How does {friend} help?

choices.label: Pass the small one to a smaller fox

steps.text: {friend} knit a long, soft sweater. The fox tried it on and twirled with shy joy in the doorway.
{friend} found a tinier fox who fit it perfectly. Both foxes paraded around with great satisfaction.
A rabbit had dug a tunnel under the garden. A small wooden sign asked visitors to please wipe their feet.
The {quest_giver} rabbit appeared at the entrance with a tiny welcome bell.
{friend} wiped, ducked, and crawled through into a soft round room full of carrot-themed art.
The rabbit served tea in {count} thimble cups. {friend} stayed until the last warm sip. The end.

templates.title: The deer who carried a lantern

steps.text: A deer with a small lantern hanging from its antler stepped softly past the porch where {friend} sat.
It paused, blinked twice, and turned its head so the lantern lit a path through the dark woods.
{friend} watched the gold glow fade between trees. The night felt friendly and tucked-in.
The dumpling had a tiny scarf.
There was a small knock at the back door. {friend} opened it and found one round dumpling, looking up hopefully.
How does {friend} respond?
The dumpling rolled over the threshold and settled on a small plate by the fire. It hummed contentedly.
'I'm Boon,' said the dumpling. {friend} nodded. They shook hand-and-pleat solemnly.
Down the lane, a pond steamed gently — it was, the {guide_mentor} confirmed, a pond made of soup.
Ducks paddled in slow circles. They tasted excellent without even meaning to.
{friend} brought a slice of bread for dipping and a small wooden bowl for a sample.
The pond was carrot. {friend} sipped, sighed, and bowed thanks. The ducks honked in chorus. The end.
The maple syrup uncorked itself politely.
Every Saturday at dawn, a parade of {count} small pancakes marched single-file across the kitchen counter.
How does {friend} celebrate?

choices.label: Lead them with a tiny banner

steps.text: {friend} waved a paper banner. The pancakes strutted along, syrup glinting like tiny medals.
{friend} drummed lightly. The pancakes high-stepped in time. The butter dish applauded with a soft clink.

templates.title: The teakettle that whistled to the moon

steps.text: On the back of the stove, a small black teakettle whistled — but only when the moon was out.
{friend} poured a cup. Steam curled up and bowed at the window, where the moon bowed back.
{friend} sipped slowly. The kettle hummed. The moon smiled wider. Everything was very, very still.

templates.title: The bread boat to the marsh

steps.text: The crust was just buoyant enough.
Down at the dock, the {quest_giver} baker had baked a boat-shaped loaf. It floated, golden and warm.
Where does {friend} sail the bread boat?
{friend} steered carefully. The herons gathered to bless the boat with three solemn nods. Crumbs were shared.
Frogs hopped aboard at every lily pad. The boat ferried each one home with a tiny wake of bread-smell.

templates.title: The jam jar that held a star

steps.text: An empty jam jar on the windowsill had quietly caught a tiny star and was glowing soft purple-pink.
The {guide_mentor} pointed out that some stars prefer cozy jars to the cold sky.
{friend} carried the jar to the bedside table and unscrewed the lid the smallest crack.
The star sighed happy and made the whole room glow like a friendly sunrise. The end.

templates.title: The muffin with a map inside

steps.text: The map had a tiny X stitched in blueberry-blue.
Inside a blueberry muffin, baked carefully by the {quest_giver}, was folded a tiny paper map.
Where does the map lead?

choices.label: To the brook's small stepping stones

steps.text: The oak waited with one leaf already pointing the way home. {friend} thanked it and walked back, content.
{friend} hopped from stone to stone. The brook giggled at each splash. At the last stone, a sparrow waved.
Steam still curled invitingly off it.
A bridge of soft uncooked noodles stretched across the creek. It was not the most reassuring bridge.
How does {friend} cross?
{friend} tiptoed. The noodles bowed beneath each step but held strong. {friend} reached the other side, beaming.
{friend} skipped lightly. The bridge bounced delighted. A small fish below applauded with a tail-clap.
On a windy hill stood a small castle entirely built of piecrust. It smelled of butter for miles.
The {quest_giver} crust-baker raised a flag of golden lattice on the highest tower.
{friend} was invited inside for a tour. The walls were warm. The drawbridge was a pie slice on hinges.
{friend} left with a small piecrust pin tucked into a pocket, and a story to keep. The end.
Bees buzzed cheerfully around its shoulder.
A honey badger knocked at the gate of {room} and asked, very politely, if there might be a spoon to spare.
How does {friend} reply?

choices.label: Lend a wooden spoon for travel

steps.text: The badger bowed. It returned the spoon the next morning with a tiny honey-stuck thank-you note.
The badger tucked it into a pocket. Three days later it returned, polished and warm with sun.

templates.title: The violin in the rabbit hole

steps.text: Down a rabbit hole, {friend} heard a tiny violin playing. The notes were no bigger than crumbs.
The {guide_mentor} rabbit was the violinist. It bowed slowly and steadily, eyes closed.
{friend} sat very still. {count} other rabbits crept in to listen, ears soft with awe.
When the song ended, every rabbit thumped its hindfoot politely, like the world's softest applause. The end.
Dew sparkled at the flower's edges.
A trumpet flower, the {needs_saving}, opened its petals and tried to play — but only a shy squeak came out.
How does {friend} encourage it?

choices.label: Cup hands as a gentle stage

steps.text: {friend} hummed a note. The flower joined in, then sang out clear and golden. Bees gathered to applaud.
{friend} cupped the air around the flower. The petals felt safer and opened wider. The note rang bright.

templates.title: The drum that taught itself to dance

steps.text: On the porch sat a small drum that, all by itself, had taught itself to dance.
It hopped left, hopped right, did a small spin. Its drumsticks waved like tiny conductor's batons.
{friend} watched, delighted. When it finished, {friend} clapped {count} times. The drum took a deep bow.
Lily pads tilted, listening.
A frog the exact shape of a small brass bell sat on a lily pad, ringing softly whenever it ribbited.
How does {friend} say hello?
{friend} tapped gently. The frog rang back. They had a small duet that turned ducks' heads.
{friend} sang. The frog rang. The pond filled with one warm, woody chord that pleased every reed.
At the foot of an old tree grew a tuft of moss that whistled lullabies whenever something tired sat near.
The {guide_mentor} explained: 'The moss has been whistling for {count} sleepy creatures since spring.'
{friend} sat down. The whistling was soft and felt like a small green hand on the heart.
{friend} stayed until the whistle slowed to a peaceful hum. Then {friend} walked home, well-rested. The end.

templates.title: The harp that flew like a kite

steps.text: Clouds gathered, curious.
A small harp, restrung with breeze, lifted off the lawn and flew like a kite, plucking itself in the wind.
What does {friend} do?
{friend} held the string carefully. The harp played a {adjective} song that drifted down through the leaves.
{friend} let go gently. The harp soared, scattering notes across the whole afternoon like bright dandelion fluff.
A kazoo, the {quest_giver}, had been hidden in a drawer for three years and was finally ready to travel.
It hummed expectantly. It had {count} planned stops, and a tiny scarf tied around its middle.
{friend} carried the kazoo carefully to the front gate.
The first stop was the postbox, which the kazoo bowed to politely.
Where does the kazoo go next?
The baker leaned out and laughed. The kazoo hummed a small song and the baker gave it a crumb of pretzel.
Children gathered. The kazoo conducted a parade in a perfect tiny circle. Everyone hummed along.

templates.title: The xylophone fence in the meadow

steps.text: The wind had already warmed up its part.
A whole fence made of brightly painted wooden xylophone bars stretched across the meadow.
How does {friend} play it?

choices.label: Tap each bar gently in turn

steps.text: {friend} ran a {prop} along the bars. A bright cascading tune leapt up, and butterflies followed each note.
{friend} tapped slowly. The fence played a stately little march that made the daisies salute.

templates.title: The tin whistle and the wind

steps.text: {friend} found a tin whistle on the windowsill. The wind was leaning in, curious.
{friend} blew softly. The wind blew softly back. Together they made a small song no one else heard.
When {friend} lowered the whistle, the wind sighed approvingly and curled up beside the curtain.
Birds tilted their heads to listen.
The {quest_giver} had a piccolo in their pocket and didn't know it. It chirped at every step.
How does {friend} help?
{friend} pointed. The {quest_giver} laughed in surprise and took it out. The piccolo played a thank-you note.
{friend} whistled. The {quest_giver} hummed. The piccolo led the way home, conducting from the pocket.

templates.title: Three friends and a braided rope

steps.text: Three friends braided a long rope together: red, blue, and yellow strands, one for each of them.
The {sidekick} pulled snug. The {helper_townsperson} smoothed the braid. {friend} tied the final knot.
They draped the rope across {room} like a banner of belonging.
