{friend} let the thread unspool and gently followed.
It crossed gardens. It crossed a sleepy street. It curled along the path home.
The other end was tied, very softly, around someone's wrist in {friend}'s own house. {friend} laughed and wrapped them in a hug. The end.
In a tucked-away corner of the castle, the {boss_mini_boss} polished a tarnished old crown.
'It's not mine,' the {boss_mini_boss} said. 'I'm just looking after it until someone deserves it.'
{friend} sat down nearby and asked what 'deserves' meant.
What does the {boss_mini_boss} answer?
{friend} offered the {boss_mini_boss} half a sandwich. The {boss_mini_boss} blinked, then chuckled. 'Yes. Like that.'
{friend} listened while the {boss_mini_boss} told a long story about their grandfather. The crown gleamed a little brighter.
It was about to pour. {friend} and the {helper_townsperson} grabbed every umbrella they could find.
They rushed to the bus stop where three kids were waiting in worried looks.
The rain began — fat warm drops splatting on the pavement.
Who gets which umbrella?

choices.label: Big polka-dot to the smallest kid

steps.text: The smallest kid disappeared under the polka-dot dome and giggled in delight. The bus splashed up just in time.
Two of the kids shared the striped one shoulder-to-shoulder. They invented a song about sharing umbrellas.
Up under the eaves, {friend} found a violin in a dusty case. The {guide_mentor} had said it was 'still asleep.'
{friend} carried it carefully down to the kitchen, where the late sun made everything golden.
The {guide_mentor} tuned each string slowly, the way you wake a small child on a Sunday.
What note does {friend} ask for first?
The note rang out like a wide-open window. Dust motes danced. The violin had remembered how to sing.
A low warm hum filled the room. {friend}'s shoulders loosened. The whole house seemed to exhale.
On a high shelf stood a small wooden horse, ears tilted forward and tail mid-swish.
{friend} lifted it down. Up close, the carving was so fine you could see tiny mane hairs.
Then the horse twitched, just once, in {friend}'s hand.
Where does the horse want to go?

choices.label: Around the rim of the table

steps.text: {friend} carried the horse outside. In the cool grass it broke into a tiny, joyous gallop, mane flying.
The horse trotted three perfect circles around a teacup, then bowed deeply to {friend} and went still again.
A folded piece of paper drifted past {friend} on a warm breeze.
Inside, the handwriting was careful and round: 'To whoever catches this — have a good day.'
{friend} smiled and slid the note into a pocket.
All afternoon, every small good thing felt sent on purpose.
At dusk, {friend} wrote a fresh note and tossed it gently up. The breeze took it eastward, off to find another finder. The end.
Every Tuesday, a tiny train of apple carts rolled past the orchard at dawn.
{friend} had a ticket — a real one, hand-stamped by the orchard {helper_townsperson}.
The train moved at the pace of a slow walker, perfect for noticing things.
What does {friend} notice on the way?

choices.label: A {adjective} fox napping under a tree
A robin teaching a sparrow to sing

steps.text: {friend} waved. The fox flicked one ear and went back to sleep. The carts squeaked on. Perfect.
{friend} listened all the way to the next stop. The sparrow nailed the last note. {friend} clapped softly.
{friend} found a single brass button on the sidewalk, polished to a gleam.
It was warm — as if it had just come off a coat.
The button told a story when held against the ear: salt wind and gull cries and old sails snapping.
What kind of voyage was the button on?
The button had ridden on the coat of a brave captain who pulled three sailors from a storm. It glowed warm again.
The button had crossed the gentle gulf with a coat that smelled of lemon and rope. It hummed softly to itself.

templates.title: The clay bird that learned to sing

steps.text: {friend} shaped a little clay bird and brought it to the {guide_mentor}.
'It needs one more thing,' the {guide_mentor} said. 'Listen to a real bird for a long time.'
{friend} sat in the garden and listened until the sparrow's whole melody felt familiar.
How does {friend} give the bird its voice?

choices.label: Hum the song into its open beak
Write the song on a tiny paper inside it

steps.text: The clay bird trembled, then trilled — a quiet copy of the sparrow's song that no fire had to bake.
Whenever the wind moved through the bird's hollow chest, the paper rustled out a tune. A gift forever.
An owl who could never quite stay awake at night perched outside {friend}'s window.
Every few minutes the owl's head bobbed forward, then jerked back upright.
{friend} laughed kindly and brought a soft little cushion to set on the windowsill.
The owl tucked its head onto the cushion and let out a long happy hoot of relief.
By morning the owl had drooled a little, in the cutest way an owl can drool. {friend} did not tell. The end.

templates.title: The day an elephant came to the park

steps.text: {friend} was eating a sandwich on a park bench when an actual elephant strolled in.
She tipped a polite trunk, walked to the fountain, and drank gracefully.
Then she sat down — carefully — on the grass beside {friend}.
'I don't get out much,' she sighed, 'but the weather seemed inviting.'
{friend} offered her the second half of the sandwich. The elephant declined politely and shared a peanut instead. The end.
Deep in the forest grew a perfect circle of ferns, all the same height.
{friend} stepped inside and the noises of the woods went hush.
Standing in the middle, {friend} could hear a tiny note from very far away.
What does {friend} do with the note?

choices.label: Hum it back, sending a reply

steps.text: The note shifted, surprised. A small chord swelled around the circle. Somewhere, a friend smiled.
{friend} carried the note home like a smooth stone. It would help on a quiet hard day.
On the windowsill stood a glass seahorse no taller than {friend}'s thumb.
When the moon hit it just right, the seahorse wobbled and bowed.
Tonight, it whispered, 'Could you sing me something? I miss the sea.'
What does {friend} sing?

choices.label: A pirate's chanty, slow and low

steps.text: The seahorse swayed gratefully. A faint salt smell drifted in. The seahorse said, 'There it is. There it is.'
The seahorse hummed along proudly. The two of them traded verses until the moon had drifted out of the window frame.
Past the gate, every flower in the garden hummed its own quiet tone.
Together they made a soft buzzing chord that changed slightly with the breeze.
{friend} walked the rows, listening for one flower that hummed off-key.
What does {friend} do for the off-key flower?

choices.label: Water it with a small cup
Sit and hum the right note for it

steps.text: Within minutes the flower's tone steadied. The whole garden's chord brightened, like a small chorus.
The flower listened, then matched the note exactly. {friend} smiled and the garden's harmony was whole again.
The {guide_mentor} showed {friend} a kite made of paper-thin iron.
'No one's flown this one in fifty years,' the {guide_mentor} said, 'because no one had the patience.'
{friend} took the spool and walked into a steady wind on the hilltop.
The kite lifted slow and heavy, then steadied — and rose, and rose, and finally caught a high current.
Up there, the iron kite looked like a tiny silver bird. The {guide_mentor} laughed and pumped a fist. The end.
On the shelf above the stove sat a jam jar — empty, except for words slowly piling up inside it.
Every nice thing anyone said in the house drifted into the jar like a butterfly settling.
Tonight the jar was nearly full. {friend} took it carefully down.
How does {friend} share the jar's words?

choices.label: Open the lid and let them out at dinner
Pour them slowly into another jar for later

steps.text: The kitchen filled with floating compliments — 'thoughtful,' 'patient,' 'silly,' 'kind.' Everyone laughed and caught a word.
{friend} saved them for a rainy day. Months later, on a sad afternoon, those words drifted out at the perfect moment.

templates.title: The sea monster who only knew knots

steps.text: Out beyond the harbor lived a sea monster who looked terrifying but could only tie knots.
Sailors had complained for years. The {frenemy} called it a menace. {friend} suspected otherwise.
{friend} rowed out with a basket of fresh rope.
What does the monster knot first?
The monster beamed. {friend} taught it three more knots. By sundown the {frenemy} was buying souvenir bracelets.
The monster blushed teal and presented the rope heart to {friend}. The {frenemy} swore later it had something in its eye.
The {guide_mentor} kept a tea box made of lacquered red wood with golden leaves painted on it.
Inside were six tiny tins, each labeled in a careful script.
'Pick the one,' the {guide_mentor} said, 'that matches how you feel today.'
Which tea does {friend} pick?
The tea steamed like a held breath. {friend} sipped and the day's small frustrations softened.
It tasted like the inside of a forest. {friend} sat quietly with the {guide_mentor} and didn't need to say a thing.
Sweet and bright on the tongue. {friend} grinned and the {guide_mentor} grinned back. The world felt fixable again.

templates.title: The kingdom in the morning fog

steps.text: Some mornings, when the fog rolled in thick, a tiny kingdom appeared on the hillside.
{friend} climbed up before the fog could lift, hoping to glimpse the gates today.
There they were — small, pearly, real enough to touch.
Does {friend} step inside?
A small hand pressed a token into {friend}'s palm — a foggy day pass. 'Come back next time,' a voice whispered.
Inside, three tiny figures waved back. The fog brightened around them. {friend} felt invited and seen.
On a clear night, the {guide_mentor} unrolled a star map across the porch boards.
'Pick a star,' they said. 'Any one. We'll travel to it in our minds.'
{friend} chose a small bright dot near the horizon.
Eyes closed, they imagined a long quiet ship sailing the dark between worlds.
When they opened their eyes, the porch was the same — but the night somehow felt bigger and kinder. The end.
In the tide pool lived an octopus named Bell, who held a small brush in each of her eight arms.
She painted on shells and gave them to passing crabs as thank-you gifts.
{friend} crouched at the edge of the pool and watched, very still.
Does Bell paint a shell for {friend}?

choices.label: Yes — a shell with a tiny rainbow

steps.text: Bell handed over the shell with two careful arms. The rainbow was perfect — eight colors instead of seven.
Bell signed the back with a tiny B and a tiny tentacle squiggle. {friend} pressed the shell over a heartbeat.

templates.title: The pumpkin that wanted to be a coach

steps.text: In the field stood the largest pumpkin {friend} had ever seen. It cleared its throat politely.
'I have always wanted to be a coach,' it confessed. 'But I have no idea where to start.'
{friend} sat down. 'Maybe we should try imagining wheels first?'
What kind of wheels?
The pumpkin sprouted dandelion wheels and rolled three feet, beaming. 'Adequate!' it cried.
The pumpkin clicked into motion with tiny gears, glided down the row, and curtsied at the corner.
The {quest_giver} placed a small quartz wand in {friend}'s hands.
'It only works for one thing,' they said. 'It can make any sad moment slightly easier.'
{friend} tucked the wand close. That very afternoon, a chance came.
A neighbor sat on the step, looking like the day had been long.
How does {friend} use the wand?

choices.label: A quiet wave from across the street
A small hello with a polished joke

steps.text: The neighbor brightened a little — just enough. The wand glowed pale pink in {friend}'s pocket.
The neighbor laughed harder than the joke deserved. The wand hummed, satisfied. {friend} grinned all the way home.

templates.title: The redwood and the small voice

steps.text: {friend} stood at the foot of a redwood so tall the top was lost in mist.
A small voice came from somewhere in the bark — maybe a beetle, maybe the tree itself.
'Hello, small thing,' it said warmly. 'How is your today going?'
{friend} answered honestly, the good and the worried, like talking to a wise old hill.
The tree hummed deep and slow. By the end of the hum, {friend} was sure things would work out. The end.
Walking the beach at dusk, {friend} found a starfish glowing faintly in the wet sand.
It was a stranded starfish, far from the receding tide. It pulsed gentle blue light.
{friend} cupped it carefully in two hands and walked toward the water.
How does {friend} return it?

choices.label: Carry it past the waves to deeper water
Place it just at the foamy edge

steps.text: The starfish brightened all five arms in thanks. A perfect five-pointed light vanished into the cool dark.
A small wave swept the starfish up and away. As it tumbled, it spelled out a tiny 'thank you' in the foam.
Past the orchard grew a thorny thicket where lost things ended up.
Hairpins, single mittens, an old toy car, a paper crown — all caught politely in the brambles.
{friend} carried gardening gloves and a small basket.
What does {friend} rescue first?
{friend} returned the crown to the kid down the lane, who lit up. 'I have been the lost king all week!'
{friend} matched it to the mate at the lost-and-found at school. The owner clapped happily and hugged the gloves.
The {guide_mentor} sat on the porch tuning a tiny ukulele, humming under their breath.
{friend} sat down beside them and waited.
'Tonight,' the {guide_mentor} said, 'I will play you any song you want, as long as I remember it.'
What does {friend} ask for?

choices.label: A song from before {friend} was born
A song the {guide_mentor} just made up

steps.text: The {guide_mentor} closed their eyes and played a melody from forty summers ago. The porch went still and warm.
Four chords later, the brand-new song was named 'Porchlight Tuesday' and would be sung again on many quiet evenings.

templates.title: The violet who came to visit

steps.text: On the kitchen window, a small violet leaned in like it was peeking through.
'Hello there,' said the violet, surprising everyone except the cat.
It had come, apparently, for a chat and a sip of cool water.
What does {friend} chat with the violet about?

choices.label: How to tell good rain from bad

steps.text: The violet recommended the east window strongly. It had opinions. It came back next Wednesday too.
By the third raindrop the violet was an expert. {friend} took notes, charmed. The cat purred along.
In a clearing of the woods stood a stone well that listened, never spoke.
{friend} had heard you could tell it anything and it would keep the secret forever.
{friend} leaned over and whispered the worry that had been knotted up all week.
The well held the worry the way a careful hand holds a small bird.
Walking home, {friend} felt about three pounds lighter and noticed the smell of pine on the wind. The end.
At the music store, the {helper_townsperson} pointed out a xylophone with only one working note.
Customers passed it by. But the note rang clear and golden.
{friend} tapped it. Bonggg. The whole store paused for a second to enjoy.
What does {friend} do for the one-note xylophone?

choices.label: Buy it and give it the place of honor
Help fix all the other bars

steps.text: {friend} brought it home and rang it every morning before breakfast. The house woke up gently every day.
Hours of patient tuning later, the xylophone played a whole scale. The store filled with bystanders' surprised smiles.

templates.title: The yellow door at the end of the lane

steps.text: At the very end of the lane stood a tiny house with a bright yellow door.
{friend} had walked past it a hundred times. Today, the door was open a crack.
Inside lived the {guide_mentor}, who had been waiting patiently to be discovered.
Over tea, they swapped stories the way old friends do, even though they had only just met.
Walking home, {friend} memorized the route. There would be many more Tuesdays at the yellow door. The end.
{friend} sat with a small zither on the back step, picking out a slow tune.
A pale moth circled in and landed on the topmost string, fluttering softly.
Every time {friend} played, the moth's wings buzzed along, adding a soft tremor to the notes.
What does {friend} play next?

choices.label: A bright marching tune for the moth

steps.text: The moth tap-danced lightly along the strings. The yard filled with a tiny, ridiculous parade of one moth and one player.
The moth went very still and listened. By the last note its wings were folded. The whole garden seemed to be holding its breath.
On the back of {friend}'s hand, a single ladybug paused to do a stretching routine.
First a tiny lunge. Then a flip. Then a perfect handstand on three legs.
{friend} clapped softly so as not to scare her.
She finished her routine, bowed, and flew off doing one last loop in the air. The end.
The {helper_townsperson} handed {friend} a small sack of birdseed. 'There's a treasure hidden in it,' they said.
'Scatter it slowly and the treasure will find you.'
{friend} walked the garden, sprinkling the seed.
Where does {friend} scatter the seed?
Six sparrows came, then ten. Then a robin, then two. The treasure was them — a yard alive with tiny visitors.
A cardinal landed first, then a chickadee, then a song sparrow. {friend} sat very still in the bright morning.
Every night the castle kitchen was tidied. Every morning, it was a disaster again.
The {quest_giver} asked {friend} to spend the night and find out who.
{friend} hid behind the {prop} with a flashlight and a notebook.
Who is messing up the kitchen?

choices.label: The royal corgi searching for snacks

steps.text: Caught red-pawed! The corgi got a designated snack basket and tidied up after itself from then on.
The ghosts apologized in see-through whispers. {friend} taught them a few tidying songs and they got the hang of it.
Before sunrise, the {guide_mentor} woke {friend} for a hot-air balloon ride.
The basket creaked. The burner thumped. Slowly the world dropped away below.
They drifted over fields and roofs, and over a fox crossing a sleepy lane.
The sun came up gold over the hill. Even the cows looked up to watch.
Coming down soft in a wide meadow, {friend} stepped out feeling like the morning belonged just to them. The end.
A breathless {quest_giver} pressed an envelope into {friend}'s hand. 'URGENT,' it said in red.
'Open it precisely at three minutes past noon,' they whispered, then ran off.
{friend} watched the clock with great seriousness.
What's inside the envelope?

choices.label: A coupon for an ice cream cone
A note that just says 'good job'

steps.text: The shop down the street was waiting. {friend} got two scoops. The {quest_giver} winked from the window.
{friend} blinked, then laughed. Maybe that was urgent — sometimes a kid needs to hear it on a random Tuesday.
On the bedside table, the goldfish in the bowl waved a fin at the rising moon.
Tonight, the moon waved back — a tiny silver fin flickering in the sky.
{friend} sat up to look more carefully. The moon really was waving.
Does {friend} say goodnight?

choices.label: Wave to both moon and fish

steps.text: Both waved back, fish and moon, and the room grew warm with shared silliness.
The moon dipped a bow. The fish blew three bubbles. The whole sky and bowl seemed quietly content.
Out in the bay, an old red buoy bobbed in the chop. It had been there for sixty years.
{friend} rowed out with the harbor {helper_townsperson} to give the buoy a fresh coat of paint.
Up close, the buoy creaked a thank-you in a wobbly old voice.
'I've guided so many home,' it said. 'No one has ever just visited.'
{friend} painted carefully and listened to the buoy's stories. By the time they rowed back, the buoy was the brightest thing on the water. The end.
The {guide_mentor} taught {friend} to make a tiny compass out of a single horsehair and a button magnet.
It worked. The hair always pointed the same way no matter how {friend} turned.
'Now,' said the {guide_mentor}, 'follow it until it gets bored.'
{friend} walked across the yard, into the orchard, until the hair gave up at the base of a chestnut.
Under the leaves: a small carved wooden coin with a smiley face on one side. A treasure for someone who looked. The end.
Hanging from the eaves were a row of icicles of different lengths.
When the wind blew right, they rang together — like a tiny choir.
{friend} held very still and listened to their slow shimmery song.
What does {friend} do?

choices.label: Make a request by tapping one gently

steps.text: The icicles slowed and matched {friend}'s voice. The choir and the kid sang a hush together as the snow began.
The longest icicle rang first, then each in turn — a perfect descending scale just for {friend}.
Above the door, a little sign read: 'Jeweler. Also fixes feelings, by appointment.'
{friend} pushed open the door. The {helper_townsperson} looked up from a tray of tiny gemstones.
'I have a worry,' said {friend}, 'and it sort of clinks around in my chest.'
How does the jeweler help?

choices.label: Polish the worry like a stone
Set it into a tiny ring to keep close

steps.text: The {helper_townsperson} buffed the worry until it shone smooth. It still existed — but it didn't poke anymore.
The little ring held the worry safely outside. {friend} could put it on or off whenever needed. Mostly off.
The {guide_mentor} took a small wooden box down from the shelf.
'These are the things I've kept,' they said. 'Each one is small and means a lot.'
Inside: a pressed leaf, a chipped marble, a folded note, a single button.
The {guide_mentor} told {friend} the story behind each one — short, plain, true.
{friend} went home and started a small box of their own. It would be sweetly full by the time {friend} was the {guide_mentor}'s age. The end.
{friend} noticed a single golden thread sticking out of a coat pocket.
Pulling it gently, it kept coming and coming — much longer than any pocket could hold.
Then a tiny voice said, 'That's the thread of luck. Don't pull too hard, or you'll use it all up at once!'
What does {friend} do?

choices.label: Tuck the thread back in carefully
Snip off just one tiny bit and save the rest

steps.text: The pocket sealed up. {friend}'s afternoon went smoothly — a nice tiny streak of small good things.
{friend} carried the snippet in a tiny envelope. It would do its quiet work on an important day next month.
In the old museum, the marble floor in the second hall hummed gently if you stood very still.
{friend} closed both eyes and listened through both feet at once.
The hum changed with each room. Each wing had its own quiet song.
Which wing's song does {friend} like best?

choices.label: The painters' hall, low and warm
The sculptors' garden, bright and tinkling

steps.text: The hum filled {friend} chest-deep. The colors on the walls seemed to deepen as if they recognized a listener.
Tiny clear notes rang along the stones. {friend} grinned at a small marble bird that almost seemed to chirp back.
The {guide_mentor} laid out a quilt made of woven nettle fibers — softer than it sounded.
'This quilt keeps lonely people from feeling alone,' they said. 'But only if it's been used.'
{friend} took a corner. The {guide_mentor} took the opposite corner.
Who needs the quilt next?

choices.label: An elderly neighbor down the street
A kid moving to a new school

steps.text: They folded the quilt and delivered it with a knock and a smile. By morning the neighbor's porch looked alive with visitors.
{friend} gave the kid a corner to hold. By the third day, they had made a new friend just from the quilt's warmth alone.
A small grey owl flew down to the window of {room} with a letter in his beak.
It was the night post — only for the most important letters.
{friend} opened the envelope. Inside, in shaky old handwriting: 'I think of you often.'
What does {friend} send back?

choices.label: A pressed leaf with one short word

steps.text: The owl took the leaf carefully and was off. Somewhere, a grandparent would open the envelope and beam.
The owl tucked the drawing under one wing. A whole evening later, an elderly hand held it up to a window and smiled.
On the couch in {room}, the pillows had risen up and crowned the {frenemy} as their captain.
{friend} found a tiny pillow flag stuck into the largest cushion.
'Surrender all your snacks!' the {frenemy} declared, fairly politely.
How does {friend} respond?
Negotiation succeeded. {friend} was allowed to join the crew with the rank of First Snack Officer.
The pillow pirates fell apart laughing. The {frenemy} surrendered with one hand on a pillow and one held up high.
At dawn, the first train of the day pulled in nearly empty.
{friend} sat by the window and watched the fields stream past.
A heron stood ankle-deep in a pond, indifferent to the world hurtling by.
The light turned from grey to gold while a single farmer waved at the train. {friend} waved back.
By the time the train reached the city, the day felt like a clean page. The end.
At the river's bend, {friend} and the {sidekick} took off their shoes and waded in.
The {sidekick} had heard a rumor about a treasure stuck under a flat smooth stone.
{friend} felt around carefully with toes — the water was cold and bright.
What does {friend} find?

choices.label: An old silver fork from somebody's picnic

steps.text: They each held it for a moment. The river hummed faintly around their ankles, like it approved.
{friend} polished it later that evening. A perfectly serviceable spaghetti fork — and a story to go with it.
Down a narrow lane stood a shop that sold things that stood still — paperweights, stones, ribbons that didn't flutter.
The {helper_townsperson} greeted {friend} from behind a counter of unmoving items.
'A stand-still thing is good,' they said, 'when your week has been too busy.'
What does {friend} pick?
All week the paperweight sat on {friend}'s desk. It steadied papers and somehow steadied {friend} too.
The stone went in {friend}'s pocket. Anytime the day got loud, {friend} closed a hand around it and breathed.
Behind the cottage, someone had planted a garden of thimbles. Each held a single very small flower.
The smallest gardener {friend} had ever seen tended them: a beetle in a tiny apron.
The beetle saluted {friend} with a leaf.
Which thimble flower is in bloom?
{friend} stayed for ten quiet minutes. The forget-me-not seemed brighter than any in any normal garden.
The beetle plucked a single petal and pressed it gently into {friend}'s palm. {friend} kept it under a clear stone forever.
Under the bed, instead of dust, lived a tiny library run by a very tidy mouse.
{friend} peeked under and the mouse stamped a small brown card: 'WELCOME, READER.'
Three shelves, twelve books, all the size of {friend}'s thumb.
Which book does {friend} borrow?

choices.label: The one about a brave moth
The one about a long quiet sea

steps.text: {friend} read by the light of the nightlight. Halfway through, the brave moth turned out to be friends with the mouse.
Each tiny page rocked like a slow tide. {friend} fell asleep with the book on the pillow, dreaming of ports.
Hanging from a wide oak grew a thick green vine, naturally knotted at the end.
{friend} grabbed hold and tested it carefully — strong as old rope.
A few small flowers on the vine giggled as they were tugged.
{friend} pushed off and swung in a wide easy arc, hair flying, laughing out loud.
When the swing slowed, {friend} sat in the grass and stared up at the leaves. The afternoon went on forever in the best way. The end.
The window seat in {room} had a small cushion that always seemed extra soft on hard days.
Tonight had been a hard day. {friend} curled up there and watched the rain.
The cushion shifted just slightly, like a quiet shoulder to lean on.
{friend} said nothing for a long time. The window seat said nothing back, perfectly.
When the rain finally eased, {friend} got up feeling lighter, the way you do after being heard. The end.
Down at the field, six tiny zeppelins were getting ready for a low slow race.
{friend} and the {frenemy} each had one — silver and red, respectively.
The flag dropped. The zeppelins drifted off the line at the pace of a polite walk.
What happens halfway down the course?

choices.label: Both zeppelins drift sideways and tangle ropes

steps.text: The {frenemy} crossed the finish first. {friend} cheered, genuinely. The {frenemy} insisted next race they'd swap balloons.
Both crews helped untangle the ropes, laughing. Officially, the race was a tie — and that was perfect.
{guide_mentor} blows up a shiny balloon in {room}. "Ready to shrink down INSIDE the balloon? My magic bubble keeps you safe. Hold on tight!"
POOF — you are tiny! Helium atoms float all around you like soft bouncy beach balls. They don't bump hard; they just drift.
{guide_mentor} smiles. "Where do you want to look first?"

choices.label: Peek at one helium atom up close
Float to the top of the balloon

steps.text: One helium atom drifts close. Two tiny electrons spin around a calm little nucleus — {guide_mentor} says they hold on tight, so helium never grabs friends.
You bob up to the top of the balloon. Helium is so light it pushes the whole balloon up into the air!
You bounce off five, ten, twenty helium atoms — boing, boing! They tickle but never stick.
{guide_mentor} pops you back to normal size. "Helium is the floaty quiet kid. Now you know what its inside feels like."

templates.title: Riding an oxygen molecule into the lungs

steps.text: {guide_mentor} taps a sparkly wand. "Take a deep breath. We are shrinking small enough to ride an oxygen molecule on the way in!"
POOF — tiny! Two oxygen atoms hold hands right next to you. They float into your nose with a soft whoosh.
The oxygen pair zooms toward the lungs. Where do you want to ride along?

choices.label: Hop onto a red blood cell

steps.text: A round red blood cell scoops up the oxygen — and you with it. Off you zoom through a sparkly pink tunnel toward the heart!
You bounce around inside a tiny lung pocket. Oxygen atoms tap-tap-tap on the walls, then slip through to go feed the body.
{guide_mentor} pops you back. "Oxygen is the everywhere helper — every breath you take is a tiny delivery!"
{guide_mentor} opens a soft glowing cloud in {room}. "This is a baby-star nursery. The cloud is made of hydrogen — the very first element! Want to peek inside?"
POOF — you shrink into the cloud. Hydrogen atoms twinkle all around you like fireflies in a pillow fort.
The atoms drift closer together. "Where do you want to play?" {guide_mentor} asks.

choices.label: Cuddle two atoms into a pair
Watch a brand-new star light up

steps.text: Two hydrogen atoms hold hands and become H2 — a tiny molecule! They wiggle together like a buddy team.
A whole bunch of hydrogen cuddles SUPER close. POP — they light up into a brand-new baby star, warm and bright!
You float gently through the cozy cloud. Every twinkle is one hydrogen atom — the simplest atom there is, just one electron and one proton.
{guide_mentor} catches you in her magic bubble. "Hydrogen built the stars AND it builds water. Pretty amazing for such a tiny atom!"
{guide_mentor} points at a bright sign humming in {room}. "That orange-red glow? That is neon gas. Let's shrink inside!"
POOF — you are inside the glass tube! Neon atoms drift all around you. Then BZZT — electricity tickles them, and they GLOW orange-red.
"Where should we peek?" {guide_mentor} asks. "The whole place is lit up!"

choices.label: Watch one atom glow up close

steps.text: One neon atom soaks up the electricity, then BLINK — out comes a tiny burst of orange light. It does that over and over!
You surf along a glowing curve of the sign. Every loop and squiggle is one long tube full of happy glowing neon.
In the bright middle, millions of neon atoms flash at once. It feels like dancing in a sunset.
{guide_mentor} pops you out. "Neon is the quiet kid who shines when you give it a little zap."
{guide_mentor} unscrews a pretend lightbulb. "Inside this bulb is argon gas — it hushes everything so the filament can shine forever. Want to peek inside?"
POOF — you are floating inside the bulb! Argon atoms drift past you, slow and calm. They do not bump, do not grab. It is very quiet.
In the middle of the bulb, a thin filament wire stretches across. "Where to?" {guide_mentor} whispers.
You tiptoe close. The filament glows orange. Argon tucks all around it like a soft scarf — no oxygen allowed, no burning out.
You drift to a quiet corner of the bulb. Argon never rushes. It just floats and protects.
{guide_mentor} flips the switch off and pops you out. "Argon's whole job is to be calm and protect. That is a noble gas's superpower."

templates.title: Skating across a mercury silver river

steps.text: {guide_mentor} holds up a pretend silver thermometer. "The shiny silver stuff inside is mercury — a metal that flows like water. We never touch it for real, but my magic bubble keeps us safe to look!"
POOF — you are tiny, inside a glass bubble, floating on a shimmery silver river of mercury. The atoms slide past each other like fish.
"Where do you want to glide?" {guide_mentor} asks, holding the bubble steady.

choices.label: Look at one mercury atom up close
Watch a droplet roll into a ball

steps.text: One mercury atom drifts past — a heavy round ball, full of electrons. It does not grab the other atoms very tight, which is why mercury flows so easily.
You glide along the shiny surface. It is so smooth it works like a mirror — you see your bubble reflected!
A mercury droplet rolls itself into a perfect silver ball, then BOING — bounces on the river without splashing. Mercury is weird and wonderful.
{guide_mentor} pops the bubble back to {room}. "Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature. Beautiful — but only safe to look at."
{guide_mentor} catches a raindrop on her finger. "Every water drop is made of TWO things — oxygen and hydrogen, holding hands. Shall we shrink inside?"
POOF — splash! You are inside the droplet. Tiny water molecules zip past you. Each one is one oxygen atom with two hydrogen buddies, like a Mickey-Mouse head.
"Where shall we swim?" {guide_mentor} asks, paddling beside you in her own bubble.

choices.label: Ride a wave inside the drop

steps.text: One water molecule drifts close. The big oxygen atom holds two tiny hydrogens at a wide angle — like a kid with two pigtails!
You surf a tiny wave inside the droplet. Water molecules slide past each other — that's why water flows so smoothly.
You bump along with a huge crowd of water molecules. They grab each other softly with their hydrogens — that grabby team is what makes water sticky!
{guide_mentor} pops the drop back into the sky. "Water is two of the simplest atoms working as a team. Hydrogen and oxygen — best buddies in nature."
{guide_mentor} sets a frosty pretend flask in {room}. "Inside is liquid nitrogen — nitrogen squeezed so cold it turned into a pool! My magic bubble keeps you snuggly warm."
POOF — you skate onto the surface of the chilly pool. Nitrogen molecules huddle in pairs, sliding past each other like dancers in fluffy mittens.
Misty fog curls up off the surface. "Where to?" {guide_mentor} grins.

choices.label: Watch a fog cloud puff up

steps.text: A puff of nitrogen escapes the pool and turns into fog. The liquid is so cold, even normal air gets frosty above it!
You glide across the smooth surface. Underneath, billions of nitrogen pairs slip and slide — that is what makes a liquid a liquid.
{guide_mentor} pops you back to {room}. "Nitrogen is everywhere in the air — but get it cold enough and it becomes a flowing pool. So cool!"

templates.title: Inside a gold coin from a treasure chest

steps.text: {guide_mentor} opens a pretend treasure chest in {room}. "Pirates love gold — but have you ever been INSIDE a coin? Shrink with me!"
POOF — you stand on top of a glowing yellow city. Gold atoms are stacked in neat rows like building blocks. Electrons swim between them like fish in a shiny sea.
"Where to first?" {guide_mentor} asks, holding up a tiny lantern.
You walk between the stacks. Gold atoms are heavy, calm, and lined up in a perfect grid — that's why gold feels solid and never rusts.
WHEEE — you ride a wave of free electrons! They flow all over the gold, which is why gold is so shiny and conducts electricity.
You climb to the surface. Light bounces off so well you can see your reflection wiggle in the gold!
{guide_mentor} pops the chest closed. "Gold is stacked atoms with a sea of electrons on top. That's why it glitters forever."
{guide_mentor} sets a chunky iron horseshoe on the table. "Iron is the strongest helper metal. Watch — it can be magnetic! Want to shrink inside?"
POOF — you are standing inside the horseshoe! Iron atoms tower around you in neat rows. Invisible swirly field lines arc from end to end.
"Where to first?" {guide_mentor} twirls in the swirly field.
WHOOSH — you grab onto a glowing field line and curve from one end of the horseshoe to the other. That is the path a paperclip would feel.
You tap one iron atom. Its tiny electrons spin in the same direction as its neighbours — that team spinning is exactly what makes iron magnetic!
You sprint from one tip of the horseshoe to the other. One tip is North, the other is South — and the pull between them is the magnetism!
{guide_mentor} sets you back on the table. "Iron's atoms can team up to make a magnetic field. Tough AND magnetic — what a hero metal!"
{guide_mentor} holds up a sparkly pretend diamond. "This is carbon — the SAME stuff as pencil lead, but stacked differently! Let's shrink inside."
POOF — you stand in a sparkly hall of crystal. Carbon atoms hold each other tight in a pattern shaped like tiny pyramids — over and over forever!
"Where do you want to wander?" {guide_mentor} twinkles.
You climb a pyramid of four carbon atoms holding hands. Each atom grabs four neighbours — that strong grip is why diamond is the HARDEST thing on Earth.
A sunbeam zooms in and PING — bounces off the carbon atoms in a hundred directions. That is the sparkle you see from outside!
You hop atom to atom like a hopscotch grid that goes in every direction at once. Carbon is the king of shapes — it builds diamonds AND pencils AND you!
{guide_mentor} pops you out. "Same atom, different stacking — that's all the difference between a pencil and a diamond. Carbon is magic."
{guide_mentor} sprinkles a single grain of salt on her hand. "Salt is sodium holding hands with chlorine. Today we shrink inside to meet the sodium side!"
POOF — you stand inside a tidy salt cube. Sodium atoms and chlorine atoms sit in checkerboard rows, holding hands tightly: Na, Cl, Na, Cl...
"Where do you want to peek?" {guide_mentor} asks.
You walk up to one sodium atom and high-five it. It gave away one tiny electron to its chlorine partner — that's how the two atoms hold so tight.
You hop down a row: sodium, chlorine, sodium, chlorine. It is the same pattern in every direction — that is why salt grains look like little cubes!
{guide_mentor} pops you back to {room}. "Sodium loves to share an electron with chlorine. Together they make salt — and salt makes food yummy!"
{guide_mentor} unscrews a pretend toy battery. "Inside is lithium — the lightest metal! It is what makes your tablet light up. Want to peek?"
POOF — you stand in a glowing little factory. Tiny lithium atoms zip from one side of the battery to the other, dropping electrons off as they go.
"Where do you want to ride?" {guide_mentor} grins.
You hop on a lithium atom and surf across the battery. It drops off one tiny electron at the other end — that electron will go power your toy!
WHEEE — a stream of electrons zooms out one end of the battery, around a wire, and back in the other. That's what makes things light up!
You visit the charging side. When the battery plugs in, the lithium atoms zip BACK the other way, getting ready to do it all over again.
{guide_mentor} pops you back to {room}. "Lithium is tiny and feathery — and it carries the power that runs your favourite gadgets."
{guide_mentor} polishes a shiny silver spoon. "Silver is the SHINIEST metal of them all. Shall we shrink inside?"
POOF — you stand on a glistening silver field. Silver atoms are stacked in tidy rows, with extra electrons skating across the top like ice dancers.
"Where do you want to glide?" {guide_mentor} asks.
You join the skating electrons. They glide across the whole spoon like one big team — which is why silver is even better than gold at carrying electricity!
A sunbeam lands on the surface. PING — it bounces off almost perfectly! That's why a silver mirror is the sharpest mirror of all.
You walk between rows of silver atoms. They are softer than gold but lined up just as neatly — that is why a silversmith can hammer them into any shape.
{guide_mentor} pops you out. "Silver: the shiniest and fastest at carrying electricity. A real superstar metal."
{guide_mentor} sets a shiny copper penny on her palm. "Copper is the warmest pinky-orange metal. It carries electricity and heat. Want to shrink in?"
POOF — you stand in a glowing orange canyon. Copper atoms line the walls in cozy rows, and warm electrons ripple over them like a sunset on water.
"Where to first?" {guide_mentor} rubs her hands warm.
WHEEE — you zoom along a copper wire as electrons flow around you. Copper is everywhere inside houses, carrying power to every lamp and screen!
You hold still and feel warmth wash through. Copper's atoms pass heat from one to the next really fast — that is why pots and pans like a copper bottom.
You climb to the surface. The pinky-orange glow is copper's special colour — only copper and gold are metals that aren't silvery-grey!
{guide_mentor} pops you back to {room}. "Copper: warm, pinky-orange, and the wiring of the whole world."
